title: Weekly coop cleaning routine
subtitle: The half-hour job that keeps a small flock healthy through the year.
excerpt: A weekly coop clean sits between the daily egg collection and the annual deep clean. Fresh perches, dry nesting boxes, a turned dust bath, and a check of every bird in hand. A walk through what the half-hour looks like and what to record on the way back to the house.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code for Laying Hens (statutory UK welfare code). AHDB Poultry small-flock husbandry notes. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, weekly poultry rounds. River Cottage Handbook 13 (Chickens), routine husbandry chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A coop floor management method where fresh wood shavings or chopped straw are added on top of soiled bedding rather than stripped out, letting the layer compost gently underfoot through the season.
The single opening under a hen's tail through which she lays eggs and passes droppings. A clean, pink, slightly moist vent is the visible sign of a healthy laying bird.
Dermanyssus gallinae, a parasitic mite that lives in coop crevices by day and feeds on the birds at night. Leaves a grey ash residue in cracks and a slow drop in laying.
A hen sitting tight on the nest to try to hatch eggs, with raised hackles and a hot bare patch on her belly. Stops laying for the duration and resists being lifted off the nest.

recipeTools.notes: Or the treadle feeder, whichever the flock uses.

content.text: A weekly clean sits between two other jobs. The daily round opens the pop-hole, tops up feed and water, glances at every bird, and collects eggs. The annual deep clean strips the coop out to bare wood in spring. Once a week, in the middle, takes about half an hour and is the single most useful piece of routine work a small flock asks for.

What the weekly round catches is the slow drift. A perch that has worked loose from its bracket, a nest box that has gone damp at the back corner, the first traces of red mite in a roof joint. Caught at the weekly round, each of these takes two minutes. Caught a month later, each is a job.
When to do this

Pick the same day each week so it lands in the routine. Sunday morning works for many people; Saturday afternoon works too. Do it after the birds have gone out to the run so the coop is empty, and finish before they want to come back in to lay. Mid-morning is the easiest slot.
What you need
A muck fork and a wheelbarrow for the soiled bedding.
A fresh bale of wood shavings or chopped straw.
A dustpan brush for the corners of the nest boxes.
A small jar of diatomaceous earth (food-grade) for the dust bath and perch ends.
A jug of clean water and a stiff brush for the feeder and drinker.
Muck out the coop floor
If you run a
deep litter

system, the weekly job is a turn and a top-up rather than a strip. Rake the surface of the litter with the muck fork to break up the crust under the perches, scrape the worst of the droppings off the perch board, and add a handful or two of fresh shavings across the surface. A working deep litter smells faintly earthy. An ammonia smell means the litter is too wet or too thin; add more dry shavings and skim the wet patches off the top.

If you run a strip-out system, fork the soiled bedding into the wheelbarrow and replace with a fresh layer 5 to 10 cm deep. Either way, give the perch board a separate going-over; that is where most of the night droppings land and the wood beneath stains and softens if it is not scraped clean weekly.
Refresh the nest boxes

Lift the bedding out of each nest box into the muck wheelbarrow. Brush the corners clean with the dustpan brush. Check the back wall and the underside of the lip for grey ash residue, which is the first sign of
red mite

moving in from the perch end of the coop. Refill each box with a generous handful of clean shavings or chopped straw. Hens lay where the bedding is soft and dry; a thin or fouled nest box pushes the laying bird to the floor or out into the run.
Check the perches

Take each perch off its brackets if it lifts out, or run a hand along it if it is fixed. The underside of the perch and the bracket join are the favoured hiding places for red mite. A dusting of diatomaceous earth along the brackets and across the perch ends interrupts the mites' route from the wood to the bird. If the perch is stained with droppings, scrape it back with a hive tool or a paint scraper before refitting.
Turn the dust bath

The dust bath is where hens shed external parasites. Fork the contents over to break up the crust and remove any droppings. If the bath has gone damp from rain, replace half of the dry earth content. Add a small scoop of wood ash from the fire if you have one, and a sprinkle of food-grade diatomaceous earth across the top. A working dust bath is loose, dry, and fine. A clumpy or wet one stops getting used.
Scrub feeder and drinker

Tip out any old feed and brush the inside of the feeder clean. Old layer pellets clump in the corners and grow mould; that bottom layer is what causes a slow drop in laying that has no obvious cause. The drinker needs a stiff brush around the inside and a rinse with clean water; nipple drinkers want the valves wiped with a clean cloth so the seal is bedding-free. Refill both and stand them back on a level slab so they do not tip.
Look over each bird

Once the coop is back in order, the birds come back in to lay. Pick each one up as she settles. A short hand-check covers what the daily glance cannot: weight either side of the breast keel, crop fullness, the look of the
vent
, and a glance at the feathering on back, breast, and belly. A bare hot patch on the belly is the early sign a hen is going
broody
; pasty droppings stuck to the vent feathers want a closer look before the next round.

attrs.title: What to feel for, four things
attrs.body: 1. Breast keel. Sharp and shallow flesh on either side of the keel bone means weight loss; a round full feel either side is what you want. 2. Crop. A morning-empty crop is normal; a hard or doughy crop is a problem (see the impacted-crop tutorial). 3. Vent. Pink and clean is fine; pasty, dirty, or pale-white needs investigating. 4. Feathering. A bare patch on the back may be a cockerel mark, on the breast it may be a broody patch, on the vent it may be feather-pecking from another bird.

content.text: What you might have found

attrs.heading: Common weekly-round findings
attrs.intro: Patterns that surface during the weekly clean, and what to do about each.

items.symptom: Grey ash residue along the perch underside or in coop crevices.
items.cause: Red mite is establishing in the coop.
items.fix: Strip out and burn the bedding from under the perches and from the nest boxes. Dust the coop interior, perch, and brackets thoroughly with diatomaceous earth. Re-bed clean. Plan a deeper clean within the week.

items.symptom: A hen tight on the nest, hackles up, hot bare patch on her belly.
items.fix: Lift her out and place her on the run side of the coop. If she returns to the nest within an hour and stays each day for three days, set up a broody-breaker crate or accept that she is sitting and supply her separately with food and water.

items.symptom: Soft-shelled or shell-less egg on the coop floor.
items.cause: Calcium shortage or a young layer still settling.
items.fix: Top up the grit feeder with crushed oyster shell. If the bird is identifiable and the soft eggs persist for more than a week, separate her for a closer look.

items.symptom: A bird standing apart in the run, tail down, eyes closing.
items.cause: Something is wrong; this is the classic sick-hen posture.
items.fix: Lift her into a separate quarantine pen with food, water, and a heat source. Check crop, vent, and breast keel. If she has not perked up in 24 hours she needs further investigation.

items.symptom: A wet patch in the deep litter that smells of ammonia.
items.cause: Roof leak above the spot, or a drinker dripping nearby.
items.fix: Skim the wet bedding off the top. Find and fix the leak before re-bedding. Ammonia damages chicken lungs faster than most owners realise.

content.text: Notes on the way back

One line in the smallholding book on the way back to the house. The date, the bird count, anything noticed: the new broody, the soft-shelled egg, the loose perch bracket fixed. Over a year these one-liners become a useful record of the flock's rhythms and of when the coop needs more substantial work.
